graph.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Patch

# Set matplotlib style
plt.style.use(selected_plot["style"])

# Ensure required features exist
required_features = selected_plot["features"]
if not all(f in df.columns for f in required_features):
    # If any feature is missing, we must adjust, but still use only provided features
    available_features = [f for f in required_features if f in df.columns]
    if len(available_features) == 0:
        raise ValueError("No features from selected_plot['features'] are available in df.")
    else:
        # We'll proceed with available features, but keep features_expected unchanged
        selected_plot["features"] = available_features

# Create a copy for processing
graph_df = df[selected_plot["features"]].copy()

# Clean up column names if needed (e.g., URL encoding)
graph_df.columns = [col.replace("%3F", "?") for col in graph_df.columns]

# Derive semantic labels for clarity in plot
graph_df = graph_df.rename(columns={
    "habitat": "Habitat",
    "class": "Edibility"
})

# Map class to more readable labels
class_mapping = {"e": "Edible", "p": "Poisonous"}
graph_df["Edibility"] = graph_df["Edibility"].map(class_mapping)

# Handle missing values
graph_df.dropna(subset=["Habitat", "Edibility"], inplace=True)

# Aggregate for stacked bar plot: count of edible vs poisonous per habitat
graph_df = graph_df.groupby(["Habitat", "Edibility"]).size().reset_index(name="Count")

# Pivot for stacked bar plot
pivot_df = graph_df.pivot(index="Habitat", columns="Edibility", values="Count").fillna(0)

# Ensure all habitats are present
all_habitats = pivot_df.index.tolist()
if len(all_habitats) == 0:
    raise ValueError("No data available after filtering.")

# Create the stacked bar plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))  # Make figure large enough for readability

# Define colors
colors = {"Edible": "#4CAF50", "Poisonous": "#F44336"}

# Plot stacked bars
pivot_df.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, ax=ax, color=colors.values(), edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.5)

# Customize plot
ax.set_title("Distribution of Edible and Poisonous Mushrooms by Habitat", fontsize=16, pad=20)
ax.set_xlabel("Habitat", fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel("Count", fontsize=14)
ax.legend(title="Edibility", loc="upper right", fontsize=12, title_fontsize=14)

# Add custom legend if needed (since we use custom colors)
legend_elements = [
    Patch(facecolor=colors["Edible"], edgecolor="black", label="Edible"),
    Patch(facecolor=colors["Poisonous"], edgecolor="black", label="Poisonous")
]
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, title="Edibility", loc="upper right", fontsize=12, title_fontsize=14)

# Improve spacing
plt.tight_layout()

# Save plot
plt.savefig(graph_file_path, bbox_inches='tight', dpi=300)

# Final graph_df contains only the data used for plotting
graph_df = graph_df.reset_index(drop=True)

# Prepare graph_data
graph_data = {
    "plot_type": selected_plot["type"],
    "features_expected": selected_plot["features"],
    "features_used": ["Habitat", "Edibility"],
    "derived_features": ["Edibility"],  # Renamed and mapped
    "x": "Habitat",
    "y": "Count",
    "hue": "Edibility",
    "facet": None,
    "aggregation": "count",
    "binning": None,
    "transformations": ["rename", "map"],
    "filters": ["dropna"],
    "n_rows_input": len(df[selected_plot["features"]]),
    "n_rows_plotted": len(graph_df),
    "title": "Distribution of Edible and Poisonous Mushrooms by Habitat"
}

dataset.description: This dataset describes mushrooms in terms of their physical characteristics. They are classified into: poisonous or edible. The dataset includes descriptions of hypothetical samples corresponding to 23 species of gilled mushrooms in the Agaricus and Lepiota Family. Each species is identified as definitely edible, definitely poisonous, or of unknown edibility and not recommended. This latter class was combined with the poisonous one. The Guide clearly states that there is no simple rule for determining the edibility of a mushroom; no rule like ``leaflets three, let it be'' for Poisonous Oak and Ivy. Attributes include cap-shape, cap-surface, cap-color, bruises, odor, gill-attachment, gill-spacing, gill-size, gill-color, stalk-shape, stalk-root, stalk-surface-above-ring, stalk-surface-below-ring, stalk-color-above-ring, stalk-color-below-ring, veil-type, veil-color, ring-number, ring-type, spore-print-color, population, habitat, and class (edible or poisonous).

graph.full_description:
1) Chart type, purpose and semantic meaning  
The chart type is a box plot, as specified in `graph_data["plot_type"]`. This chart is designed to compare the distribution of the numeric variable "ring-number" (mapped from categorical values: none=0, one=1, two=2) between two groups defined by the categorical variable "class": edible ("e") and poisonous ("p").  

The purpose of the chart is to assess whether there is a statistical difference in the distribution of ring numbers between edible and poisonous mushrooms, potentially indicating a correlation between ring count and edibility. In the context of the dataset, which aims to classify mushrooms as edible or poisonous based on physical characteristics, this chart helps evaluate if ring-number is a discriminative feature.  

Semantically, the chart shows how the ring-number values are distributed within each mushroom class. Since ring-number is ordinal (none → one → two), the box plot allows comparison of central tendency, spread, and outliers between classes. The chart entails a visual summary of the data’s distributional properties across classes, helping to identify if one class tends to have more rings than the other.

2) What is plotted (variables and encodings)  
The x-axis variable is "class", which is categorical and encodes the mushroom class: "e" (edible) and "p" (poisonous). The y-axis variable is "ring-number", which has been transformed from its original categorical form ("n", "o", "t") into numeric values (0, 1, 2) using the mapping: none=0, one=1, two=2. This transformation is explicitly stated in `graph_data["transformations"]`.  

There is no hue variable (`hue`: null) and no facet (`facet`: null), meaning no additional grouping or paneling is applied. The data is split by "class" into two groups, and a separate box plot is drawn for each.  

Each box plot encodes the distribution of "ring-number" within a class: the box represents the interquartile range (IQR), the line inside the box is the median, the "whiskers" extend to the furthest points within 1.5×IQR from the quartiles, and any points outside this range are plotted as individual outliers. The box plots are drawn using `matplotlib.pyplot.boxplot` with `patch_artist=True`, meaning the boxes are filled with color.  

No aggregation, binning, or additional transformations beyond the numeric mapping are applied. The data is filtered to drop any rows with missing values in "ring-number" or "class", as stated in `graph_data["filters"]`.

3) Data shown (from graph_df)  
The `graph_df` contains 8124 rows, as reported in `graph_data["n_rows_plotted"]`. The columns present are:  
- `class` (categorical, values: "e", "p")  
- `ring-number` (numeric, values: 0, 1, 2)  

For `ring-number`:  
- Min: 0  
- Max: 2  
- Mean: 1.00 (computed from graph_df)  
- Median: 1.00 (computed from graph_df)  
- 25th percentile: 1.00  
- 75th percentile: 1.00  

For `class`:  
- Number of categories: 2  
- Top categories by frequency:  
  - "e" (edible): 4208 rows  
  - "p" (poisonous): 3916 rows  

Each box plot represents the distribution of "ring-number" within each class. Since "ring-number" is discrete (only 0, 1, 2), the box plots are based on these discrete values. The box for each class shows that the median is 1, and the IQR is 0 (since Q1 and Q3 are both 1), indicating that the majority of values in each group are 1. The whiskers extend to 0 and 2, and there are outliers at 0 and 2 in the "poisonous" group.

4) Patterns and relationships visible  
Both edible and poisonous mushrooms have a median ring-number of 1 (i.e., "one ring"). The interquartile range for both groups is 0, meaning that the middle 50% of values in each group are exactly 1. The whiskers extend to 0 and 2, indicating that values of 0 ("none") and 2 ("two") are present in both groups.  

There are two outliers visible in the chart: one at 2 in the "edible" group and one at 0 in the "poisonous" group. These outliers are individual data points that lie beyond the whiskers (which extend to 1.5×IQR from the quartiles; since IQR=0, any value ≠1 is an outlier).  

There is no visible difference in central tendency or spread between the two classes. The distributions are nearly identical, suggesting no strong association between ring-number and mushroom class. The presence of outliers in both groups does not indicate a systematic difference.  

5) Image/visual properties (from the image)  
The chart is a horizontal box plot with two boxes side-by-side. The x-axis is labeled "Mushroom Class" and has two tick labels: "Edible" and "Poisonous". The y-axis is labeled "Ring Number (0=none, 1=one, 2=two)" and ranges from 0.00 to 2.00, with tick marks at 0.25 intervals.  

The title is centered at the top: "Distribution of Ring Number (None=0, One=1, Two=2) by Mushroom Class (Edible vs Poisonous)". The chart has a white background with black gridlines (visible as light gray lines) for both axes.  

The two boxes are filled with distinct colors: the "Edible" box is a teal/greenish color, and the "Poisonous" box is a pale blue/grayish color, drawn from the `plt.cm.Set3` colormap. The boxes are of equal width and are centered over their respective x-axis labels.  

The median lines within the boxes are black and horizontal. The whiskers are thin black lines extending from the boxes to the minimum and maximum non-outlier values (0 and 2). Two individual outlier points are shown as black circles: one at (Edible, 2.0) and one at (Poisonous, 0.0).  

The layout is clean and uncluttered. There is no legend, as the x-axis labels serve to identify the groups. Tick labels on both axes are horizontal and legible. The chart uses `plt.tight_layout()`, which ensures no overlap between elements.  

6) Caveats and unknowns  
The exact color names for the boxes are approximate (teal and pale blue/gray) and cannot be precisely determined without access to the colormap values. The specific line width or alpha/transparency values are not specified in the code and are not visually inferable.  

The exact number of outliers is not determinable from the image alone; the code suggests that any value ≠1 is an outlier, but the image only shows two outliers. The full count of outliers per group is not visible.

It is unknown whether the box plot uses a specific outlier threshold (e.g., 1.5×IQR) or if the whiskers are manually set to 0 and 2. The code does not specify this, but given the discrete nature of the data, the whiskers are likely set to the min and max of the non-outlier values, which are 0 and 2.  

The dataset description mentions 23 species, but the chart does not show species-level information; it aggregates all data by class. The chart does not show any other variables (e.g., cap-shape, odor) that might influence the relationship.
graph.short_description: A box plot comparing the distribution of ring-number (none, one, two) between edible and poisonous mushrooms, to assess if ring count correlates with edibility.
graph.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import json

# Set matplotlib style
plt.style.use(selected_plot["style"])

# Extract required features
features_expected = selected_plot["features"]
df_subset = df[features_expected].copy()

# Since "ring-number" is nominal (categorical) and "class" is also nominal, 
# we need to map "ring-number" to numeric for box plot (if possible) or use a categorical box plot.
# But box plots typically require numeric data for the y-axis. Since "ring-number" is nominal, 
# we need to interpret it as ordinal or convert to numeric if categories are ordered.

# From metadata, "ring-number" is nominal. Common values: "o" (one), "t" (two), "n" (none) — likely ordinal.
# We'll map to numeric for plotting: none=0, one=1, two=2.

ring_map = {"n": 0, "o": 1, "t": 2}
df_subset["ring-number"] = df_subset["ring-number"].map(ring_map)

# Check for missing values
df_subset = df_subset.dropna(subset=["ring-number", "class"])

# Since "class" is nominal (e.g., "e" for edible, "p" for poisonous), we can use it as hue.
# We'll create a box plot of ring-number (numeric) grouped by class.

# Define derived features
derived_features = ["ring-number"]  # since we mapped the original to numeric

# Final data for plotting
graph_df = df_subset.copy()

# Ensure graph_df contains only features used
features_used = ["class", "ring-number"]
graph_df = graph_df[features_used]

# Define graph_data
graph_data = {
    "plot_type": selected_plot["type"],
    "features_expected": features_expected,
    "features_used": features_used,
    "derived_features": derived_features,
    "x": "class",
    "y": "ring-number",
    "hue": None,
    "facet": None,
    "aggregation": None,
    "binning": None,
    "transformations": ["Mapped 'ring-number' to numeric: none=0, one=1, two=2"],
    "filters": ["Dropped rows with missing values in 'ring-number' or 'class'"],
    "n_rows_input": len(df_subset.dropna(subset=["ring-number", "class"])),
    "n_rows_plotted": len(graph_df),
    "title": "Distribution of Ring Number (None=0, One=1, Two=2) by Mushroom Class (Edible vs Poisonous)"
}

# Create box plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
box_plot = ax.boxplot(
    [graph_df[graph_df["class"] == "e"]["ring-number"], 
     graph_df[graph_df["class"] == "p"]["ring-number"]],
    labels=["Edible", "Poisonous"],
    patch_artist=True
)

# Customize colors
colors = plt.cm.Set3(np.linspace(0, 1, len(box_plot["boxes"])))
for patch, color in zip(box_plot["boxes"], colors):
    patch.set_facecolor(color)

# Add title and labels
ax.set_title(graph_data["title"])
ax.set_ylabel("Ring Number (0=none, 1=one, 2=two)")
ax.set_xlabel("Mushroom Class")

# Improve layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Save plot
plt.savefig(graph_file_path)

# Clear plot to avoid memory issues
plt.close(fig)

# we need to map "ring-number" to numeric for box plot (if possible) or use a categorical box plot.
# But box plots typically require numeric data for the y-axis. Since "ring-number" is nominal, 
# we need to interpret it as ordinal or convert to numeric if categories are ordered.

# From metadata, "ring-number" is nominal. Common values: "o" (one), "t" (two), "n" (none) — likely ordinal.
# We'll map to numeric for plotting: none=0, one=1, two=2.

ring_map = {"n": 0, "o": 1, "t": 2}
df_subset["ring-number"] = df_subset["ring-number"].map(ring_map)

# Check for missing values
df_subset = df_subset.dropna(subset=["ring-number", "class"])

# Since "class" is nominal (e.g., "e" for edible, "p" for poisonous), we can use it as hue.
# We'll create a box plot of ring-number (numeric) grouped by class.

# Define derived features
derived_features = ["ring-number"]  # since we mapped the original to numeric

# Final data for plotting
graph_df = df_subset.copy()

# Ensure graph_df contains only features used
features_used = ["class", "ring-number"]
graph_df = graph_df[features_used]

# Define graph_data
graph_data = {
    "plot_type": selected_plot["type"],
    "features_expected": features_expected,
    "features_used": features_used,
    "derived_features": derived_features,
    "x": "class",
    "y": "ring-number",
    "hue": None,
    "facet": None,
    "aggregation": None,
    "binning": None,
    "transformations": ["Mapped 'ring-number' to numeric: none=0, one=1, two=2"],
    "filters": ["Dropped rows with missing values in 'ring-number' or 'class'"],
    "n_rows_input": len(df_subset.dropna(subset=["ring-number", "class"])),
    "n_rows_plotted": len(graph_df),
    "title": "Distribution of Ring Number (None=0, One=1, Two=2) by Mushroom Class (Edible vs Poisonous)"
}

# Create box plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
box_plot = ax.boxplot(
    [graph_df[graph_df["class"] == "e"]["ring-number"], 
     graph_df[graph_df["class"] == "p"]["ring-number"]],
    labels=["Edible", "Poisonous"],
    patch_artist=True
)

# Customize colors
colors = plt.cm.Set3(np.linspace(0, 1, len(box_plot["boxes"])))
for patch, color in zip(box_plot["boxes"], colors):
    patch.set_facecolor(color)

# Add title and labels
ax.set_title(graph_data["title"])
ax.set_ylabel("Ring Number (0=none, 1=one, 2=two)")
ax.set_xlabel("Mushroom Class")

# Improve layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Save plot
plt.savefig(graph_file_path)

# Clear plot to avoid memory issues
plt.close(fig)

dataset.description: This dataset describes mushrooms in terms of their physical characteristics. They are classified into: poisonous or edible. The dataset includes descriptions of hypothetical samples corresponding to 23 species of gilled mushrooms in the Agaricus and Lepiota Family. Each species is identified as definitely edible, definitely poisonous, or of unknown edibility and not recommended. This latter class was combined with the poisonous one. The Guide clearly states that there is no simple rule for determining the edibility of a mushroom; no rule like ``leaflets three, let it be'' for Poisonous Oak and Ivy. Attributes include cap-shape, cap-surface, cap-color, bruises, odor, gill-attachment, gill-spacing, gill-size, gill-color, stalk-shape, stalk-root, stalk-surface-above-ring, stalk-surface-below-ring, stalk-color-above-ring, stalk-color-below-ring, veil-type, veil-color, ring-number, ring-type, spore-print-color, population, habitat, and class (edible or poisonous).

graph.full_description:
1) Chart type, purpose and semantic meaning  
The chart type is a scatter matrix (also known as a pairplot), as specified in `graph_data["plot_type"]`. This visualization displays pairwise relationships between multiple variables in a grid format, with histograms along the diagonal and scatter plots in the off-diagonal cells. The purpose of this chart is to explore potential patterns, clusters, or separations between edible and poisonous mushrooms based on the categorical features: cap-shape, gill-size, and stalk-shape. The chart helps answer whether these features, when considered in pairs, can visually distinguish between the two classes. The semantic meaning is to provide a comprehensive visual summary of the distribution and pairwise relationships of the selected mushroom features, with the class variable used to color-code the data points, aiding in the identification of any class-specific patterns or separability. The chart entails the simultaneous examination of multiple feature combinations to assess their discriminative power for classification.

2) What is plotted (variables and encodings)  
The variables plotted are `cap-shape`, `gill-size`, `stalk-shape`, and `class`, as listed in `graph_data["features_used"]`. The `hue` variable is `class`, which is encoded as color: blue for poisonous ('p') and green for edible ('e'). The `x` and `y` variables are not explicitly defined in `graph_data` (both are `null`), as the scatter matrix plots all pairwise combinations of the selected features. The `facet` is `null`, indicating no faceting by an additional variable. The features are nominal (categorical) and have been transformed using label encoding (as noted in `graph_data["transformations"]`) to assign numerical values for plotting, though the original categorical labels are retained for axis and legend labels. The diagonal plots are histograms of each individual feature, colored by class. The off-diagonal plots are scatter plots showing the relationship between two features, with points colored by class. No aggregation, binning, or additional transformations beyond label encoding are applied.

3) Data shown (from graph_df)  
The `graph_df` contains 8124 rows, as reported in `graph_data["n_rows_plotted"]`. The columns in `graph_df` are: `cap-shape`, `gill-size`, `stalk-shape`, `class`, `cap-shape_encoded`, `gill-size_encoded`, `stalk-shape_encoded`, `class_encoded`.  
For categorical columns:  
- `cap-shape`: 6 categories ('x', 'f', 'b', 'k', 'c', 's'). Frequencies: 'x' (approx. 2000), 'f' (approx. 1600), 'b' (approx. 400), 'k' (approx. 600), 'c' (approx. 0), 's' (approx. 20).  
- `gill-size`: 2 categories ('n', 'b'). Frequencies: 'n' (approx. 2200), 'b' (approx. 5900).  
- `stalk-shape`: 2 categories ('e', 't'). Frequencies: 'e' (approx. 5500), 't' (approx. 2600).  
- `class`: 2 categories ('p', 'e'). Frequencies: 'p' (approx. 4000), 'e' (approx. 4100).  
The encoded columns (`*_encoded`) are numerical representations of the categorical variables, derived via label encoding, but their exact mappings are not provided in `graph_df` or `graph_data`. The chart is not aggregated; each point in the scatter plots represents a single observation from the dataset, and each bar in the histograms represents a bin of counts for a category or encoded value.

4) Patterns and relationships visible  
In the scatter plots (off-diagonal), the points are clustered at discrete positions corresponding to the categorical values of the features. For example, in the "cap-shape vs gill-size" plot, points are located at intersections of the cap-shape categories ('x', 'f', 'b', 'k', 'c', 's') on the y-axis and gill-size categories ('n', 'b') on the x-axis. The color (blue for 'p', green for 'e') shows the class distribution at each combination. Some combinations show a dominance of one class; for instance, in "cap-shape vs gill-size", the 'x' cap-shape with 'b' gill-size has more blue points (poisonous), while 'f' cap-shape with 'b' gill-size has more green points (edible). The histograms on the diagonal show the distribution of each feature by class. For "cap-shape", 'x' and 'f' are the most frequent, with 'x' having a higher count of edible mushrooms and 'f' having a higher count of poisonous mushrooms. For "gill-size", 'b' is dominant overall, with a higher count of edible mushrooms. For "stalk-shape", 'e' is dominant, with a higher count of edible mushrooms. The "class" histogram shows a nearly balanced distribution between 'p' and 'e'. No clear linear or continuous trend is visible due to the categorical nature of the data, but there are visible differences in the distribution of classes across feature combinations.

5) Image/visual properties (from the image)  
The chart is a 4x4 grid of plots, arranged in a square layout. The diagonal plots are histograms with bars stacked by class (blue for 'p', green for 'e'), using 10 bins as specified in the code. The off-diagonal plots are scatter plots with circular markers, colored blue for 'p' and green for 'e', with an alpha of 0.7 (semi-transparent). graph.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import cm
from matplotlib.patches import Patch
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D

# Set style
plt.style.use(selected_plot["style"])

# Extract features
features_expected = selected_plot["features"]
features_used = features_expected.copy()

# Check if all expected features exist in df
missing_features = [f for f in features_expected if f not in df.columns]
if missing_features:
    # If any feature is missing, we must adjust features_used to only include available ones
    features_used = [f for f in features_expected if f in df.columns]
    # Since we cannot use missing features, we proceed with available ones
    # But keep features_expected unchanged as per requirement

# Filter df to only include required features
df_subset = df[features_used].copy()

# Handle missing values
df_subset = df_subset.dropna()

# For scatter matrix with nominal features, we need to encode them numerically for plotting
# But we'll keep original labels for legend and titles
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

# Create a dictionary to store encoders and mappings
encoders = {}
for col in features_used:
    if df_subset[col].dtype == 'object':
        le = LabelEncoder()
        df_subset[col + '_encoded'] = le.fit_transform(df_subset[col])
        encoders[col] = le
    else:
        df_subset[col + '_encoded'] = df_subset[col]

# Add derived features to features_used
derived_features = [col + '_encoded' for col in features_used]
features_used.extend(derived_features)

# If class is in features, use it for coloring
hue_col = 'class' if 'class' in features_used else None
if hue_col:
    if hue_col in encoders:
        df_subset[hue_col + '_encoded'] = encoders[hue_col].transform(df_subset[hue_col])
    else:
        df_subset[hue_col + '_encoded'] = df_subset[hue_col]

# For scatter matrix, we need to create a pairplot-like grid
# We'll use matplotlib to create a scatter matrix manually

# Get unique combinations of features to plot
n_features = len(features_used)
n_features_encoded = len([f for f in features_used if f.endswith('_encoded')])

# We'll plot only the encoded features for scatter matrix
plot_features = [f for f in features_used if f.endswith('_encoded')]

# Create a grid for scatter matrix
fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_features_encoded, n_features_encoded, figsize=(12, 12))
for i in range(n_features_encoded):
    for j in range(n_features_encoded):
        ax = axes[i, j]
        if i == j:
            # Diagonal: histogram
            if hue_col:
                for label in df_subset[hue_col].unique():
                    subset = df_subset[df_subset[hue_col] == label]
                    ax.hist(subset[plot_features[i]], alpha=0.7, label=label, bins=10, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.5)
                ax.set_title(plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', ''))
                ax.legend()
            else:
                ax.hist(df_subset[plot_features[i]], alpha=0.7, bins=10, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.5)
                ax.set_title(plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', ''))
        else:
            # Off-diagonal: scatter
            if hue_col:
                for label in df_subset[hue_col].unique():
                    subset = df_subset[df_subset[hue_col] == label]
                    ax.scatter(subset[plot_features[j]], subset[plot_features[i]], label=label, alpha=0.7)
                ax.set_title(f"{plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', '')} vs {plot_features[j].replace('_encoded', '')}")
                ax.legend()
            else:
                ax.scatter(df_subset[plot_features[j]], df_subset[plot_features[i]], alpha=0.7)
                ax.set_title(f"{plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', '')} vs {plot_features[j].replace('_encoded', '')}")

# Set labels
for i in range(n_features_encoded):
    axes[i, 0].set_ylabel(plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', ''))
    axes[n_features_encoded - 1, i].set_xlabel(plot_features[i].replace('_encoded', ''))

# Tight layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Save plot
plt.savefig(graph_file_path, bbox_inches='tight', dpi=300)

# Create graph_df with only the final processed data used for plotting
# We need to include only the encoded features used in the plot
graph_df = df_subset[plot_features].copy()
if hue_col:
    graph_df[hue_col] = df_subset[hue_col]

# Ensure graph_df contains only features_used (which includes derived features)
# But for the final graph_df, we only need the actual plotted data
# We'll include the original categorical features for reference if needed
# But per instructions, graph_df should contain only columns in features_used
# So we'll include both original and encoded if they are in features_used
final_columns = []
for col in features_used:
    if col in df_subset.columns:
        final_columns.append(col)

graph_df = df_subset[final_columns].copy()

# Prepare graph_data
graph_data = {
    "plot_type": selected_plot["type"],
    "features_expected": features_expected,
    "features_used": features_used,
    "derived_features": derived_features,
    "x": None,
    "y": None,
    "hue": hue_col if hue_col else None,
    "facet": None,
    "aggregation": None,
    "binning": None,
    "transformations": ["Label encoding for nominal features"],
    "filters": ["Removed rows with missing values"],
    "n_rows_input": len(df),
    "n_rows_plotted": len(graph_df),
    "title": "Scatter Matrix of Mushroom Features Colored by Class"
}

# Ensure graph_df only contains columns in features_used
graph_df = graph_df[features_used]

From `graph_data`, `x` and `y` are null, indicating no specific x/y variables are designated; instead, the pairplot structure inherently uses all feature pairs. `hue` is "class", meaning the color of each point in scatterplots encodes the class (edible 'e' or poisonous 'p'). `facet` is null, so no faceting is used. - "stalk-surface-below-ring": 4 categories ('f', 'k', 's', 'y'). From the histogram: 's' ≈ 4900, 'k' ≈ 2400, 'f' ≈ 600, 'y' ≈ 200.  
- "class": 2 categories ('e', 'p'). From the scatterplots, 'p' (poisonous) appears to be slightly more frequent than 'e' (edible). Exact counts from the image cannot be determined precisely, but the distribution is close to balanced, with 'p' slightly more common.  

Each scatterplot point represents one mushroom, and each histogram bar represents the count of mushrooms with a specific surface texture value.

4) Patterns and relationships visible  
In the scatterplots:  
- For "cap-surface" vs. "stalk-surface-above-ring": Points are scattered across all combinations. 's' (smooth) in cap-surface is common with 's' in stalk-surface-above-ring, and both 'e' and 'p' classes are present. There is no clear separation by class.  
- For "cap-surface" vs. "stalk-surface-below-ring": Similar pattern: 's' in cap-surface and 's' in stalk-surface-below-ring are frequent, and both classes are present.  
- For "stalk-surface-above-ring" vs. "stalk-surface-below-ring": 's' in both is the most common combination, with both classes present. Some combinations like 'k' in above-ring and 'k' in below-ring are rare and mostly associated with 'p' class.  
In the histograms:  
- "cap-surface": 's' is most frequent, followed by 'y', then 'f', then 'g'.  
- "stalk-surface-above-ring": 's' is dominant, followed by 'k', then 'f', then 'y'.  
- "stalk-surface-below-ring": 's' is most common, followed by 'k', then 'f', then 'y'.  
There is no strong visual separation of classes in any scatterplot; both classes appear across most texture combinations. The most frequent texture is 's' (smooth) in all three features, and this is present in both classes.

5) Image/visual properties (from the image)  
The chart is a 3x3 grid of subplots arranged in a square layout. The diagonal contains histograms; off-diagonal contains scatterplots. Axes are labeled with feature names. Tick labels on categorical axes are rotated 45 degrees for readability. Gridlines are not visible. Each subplot has black borders. The legend is placed outside the rightmost column of plots, anchored at top-left, with title "Class" and two entries: pink circle for 'e', teal circle for 'p'. The title "Pairplot of Cap and Stalk Surface Textures by Class" is not visible in the image (though specified in `graph_data`). The histogram bars are skyblue with black edges and uniform width. Scatterplot points are circular, with pink for 'e' and teal for 'p', size 20, and alpha=0.7 (slightly transparent). There is minimal overlap in scatterplots due to low point density. The layout is clean, with tight spacing between subplots.

6) Caveats and unknowns  
The exact counts for each category in the histograms and for each class cannot be determined with perfect precision from the image alone; estimates are based on visual inspection. The title specified in `graph_data` is not visible in the image. The exact color names (e.g., "pink" and "teal") are approximate descriptions of the hues used. The dataset description mentions 23 species, but the chart does not display species-level information. The code uses `sns.color_palette("husl", len(class_labels))`, but the exact color values are not specified and are inferred visually. The exact distribution of the "class" variable (e.g., exact count of 'e' vs. 'p') is not determinable from the image without counting all points, which is impractical. The presence of NaNs in the original dataset is not visible in the final plot, as they were dropped. The exact meaning of the categorical codes ('f', 'g', 's', 'y', 'k') is not explained in the image or `graph_data` but is known from the dataset description (e.g., 's' = smooth, 'f' = fibrous, 'y' = youthful, 'g' = grooved, 'k' = knobby).
graph.short_description: A pairplot showing pairwise relationships between cap surface, stalk surface above ring, and stalk surface below ring, with points colored by class, to explore surface texture patterns associated with toxicity.
graph.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Set matplotlib style
plt.style.use(selected_plot["style"])

# Extract features
features_expected = selected_plot["features"]
df_subset = df[features_expected].copy()

# Check if all expected features exist
missing_features = [f for f in features_expected if f not in df_subset.columns]
if missing_features:
    # If any feature is missing, we must adjust but keep features_expected unchanged
    # We will use only available features from the expected list
    available_features = [f for f in features_expected if f in df_subset.columns]
    if len(available_features) < 2:
        # Cannot make a pairplot with less than 2 features
        # Fallback: use first 2 available features (if any) or raise error
        if len(available_features) == 0:
            raise ValueError("No features available for pairplot")
        elif len(available_features) == 1:
            # Still cannot make pairplot with 1 feature
            raise ValueError("Cannot create pairplot with only one feature")
        else:
            df_subset = df_subset[available_features]
            features_expected = available_features
else:
    # All features are present
    df_subset = df_subset[features_expected]

# Convert class to categorical for coloring
df_subset['class'] = pd.Categorical(df_subset['class'])

# Create a copy for graph_df
graph_df = df_subset.copy()

# Ensure no NaNs in the data
graph_df = graph_df.dropna()

# Define derived features (none needed for nominal pairplot)
derived_features = []

# Set up pairplot using seaborn (which uses matplotlib under the hood)
# Since we are restricted to matplotlib, we'll manually create a pairplot
# using matplotlib subplots

# Get unique features for plotting
plot_features = [f for f in graph_df.columns if f != 'class']
n_features = len(plot_features)

# Create a grid of subplots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_features, n_features, figsize=(12, 12))
if n_features == 1:
    axes = [[axes]]  # Ensure 2D structure

# Define class colors
class_labels = graph_df['class'].cat.categories
class_colors = sns.color_palette("husl", len(class_labels))

# Create a mapping from class to color
class_to_color = {cls: color for cls, color in zip(class_labels, class_colors)}

# Populate the grid
for i in range(n_features):
    for j in range(n_features):
        ax = axes[i][j]
        if i == j:
            # Diagonal: histogram or count plot
            counts = graph_df[plot_features[i]].value_counts().sort_index()
            ax.bar(counts.index.astype(str), counts.values, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.5)
            ax.set_title(f"{plot_features[i]}")
            ax.set_ylabel("Count")
            ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
            ax.set_xlabel("")
        else:
            # Off-diagonal: scatter plot
            x_vals = graph_df[plot_features[j]]
            y_vals = graph_df[plot_features[i]]
            class_vals = graph_df['class']
            for cls in class_labels:
                mask = class_vals == cls
                ax.scatter(x_vals[mask], y_vals[mask], label=cls, color=class_to_color[cls], alpha=0.7, s=20)
            ax.set_xlabel(plot_features[j])
            ax.set_ylabel(plot_features[i])
            ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
            if i == 0:
                ax.legend(title='Class', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')

# Adjust layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Save the plot
plt.savefig(graph_file_path, bbox_inches='tight', dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Prepare graph_data
graph_data = {
    "plot_type": selected_plot["type"],
    "features_expected": features_expected,
    "features_used": graph_df.columns.tolist(),
    "derived_features": derived_features,
    "x": None,
    "y": None,
    "hue": "class",
    "facet": None,
    "aggregation": None,
    "binning": None,
    "transformations": [],
    "filters": ["dropna"],
    "n_rows_input": len(df_subset),
    "n_rows_plotted": len(graph_df),
    "title": "Pairplot of Cap and Stalk Surface Textures by Class"
}

# Define graph_df as the final processed data used for plotting
graph_df = graph_df.reset_index(drop=True)
